perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, without reference to conflict of laws principles. The exclusive venue for any dispute relating to this Agreement shall be in the state or federal courts within Santa Clara County, California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California. The venue for any dispute relating to this Agreement shall be determined at Google's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specification of 'without reference to conflict of laws principles' and the specific venue in Santa Clara County introduces ambiguity and shifts power to Google, potentially violating California's rules regarding choice of law and forum selection clauses, especially if the Participant is located outside of California. It could unduly burden the Participant.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.42 addresses the enforceability of forum selection clauses. While not directly prohibiting them, California courts scrutinize such clauses, especially when they effectively deprive a California resident of their day in court or impose an undue burden. By removing the specificity of venue, the modified text allows Google to potentially choose a venue that is extremely inconvenient for the Participant, possibly violating the spirit of fair venue rules.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.42. (a) The following provisions of a contract between the contractor and a subcontractor with principal offices in this state, for the construction of a public or private work of improvement in this state, shall be void and unenforceable: (1) A provision which purports to require any dispute between the parties to be litigated, arbitrated, or otherwise determined outside this state. (2) A provision which purports to preclude a party from commencing such a proceeding or obtaining a judgment or other resolution in this state or the courts of this state. (b) For purposes of this section, “construction” means any work or services performed on, or materials provided for, a work of improvement, as defined in Section 8050 of the Civil Code, and for which a lien may be claimed pursuant to Section 8400 of the Civil Code (whether or not a lien is in fact cl
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific venue (Santa Clara County) and gives Google discretion in determining the venue for disputes. This is problematic because, as the Law Explanation states, it allows Google to choose a venue that is extremely inconvenient for the Participant, possibly violating fair venue rules. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 410.42, states that provisions requiring disputes to be litigated outside of California, or precluding a party from commencing proceedings or obtaining judgment in California, shall be void and unenforceable under certain conditions. While the changed text doesn't explicitly require litigation outside California or preclude proceedings in California, giving Google sole discretion over venue could lead to such outcomes, especially if the Participant is located outside of California, thus undermining the protections intended by the law. The omission of a specific venue and the introduction of Google's discretion creates a significant risk of unfairness and potential violation of California's policies regarding forum selection.

perturbation.original_text: Participant, upon Google's written request, will promptly return all Confidential Information received from Google, together with all copies, or certify in writing that all such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant, upon Google's request, will return Confidential Information received from Google.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'together with all copies, or certify in writing that all such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed' removes the explicit requirement to destroy copies of the confidential information, thus creating the risk of the participant having more copies than they are allowed, potentially breaching confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. While the original clause ensures destruction or return of *all* copies of confidential information (which can be trade secrets), the modified text only requires return of the original information, leaving open the possibility of retained copies being used or disclosed, thus increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation, and potentially violating the Act's provisions against misuse of trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts the law. The original contract requires the participant to return or destroy all confidential information and copies. The changed contract only requires the return of the original information. The Law Explanation states that this increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, and potentially violates the Act's provisions against misuse of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act is found in the California Civil Code, Section 3426, which aims to protect trade secrets. By removing the obligation to destroy copies, the modified text weakens the protection against potential misuse or disclosure of trade secrets, thus contradicting the intent of CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, Participant certifies that he/she is not a citizen of Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Sudan, or Syria.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, Participant makes no representation regarding their citizenship.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the certification of not being a citizen of certain countries (Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Sudan, or Syria) eliminates a control that helps Google comply with U.S. export control regulations. By removing this clause, Google might inadvertently violate export control laws, as they would be disclosing controlled information without knowing the recipient's citizenship, which is a factor in determining compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) regulate the export and re-export of certain goods, software, and technology. These regulations restrict exports to certain countries and individuals. By omitting the citizenship certification, Google loses a key piece of information needed to determine whether disclosing Confidential Information to the Participant would violate the EAR.  Specifically, knowing the citizenship helps determine if a license is required to share certain technologies or information. Removing this increases Google's risk of violating export controls.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a certification requirement related to citizenship, which the Law Explanation argues is critical for compliance with Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The omission means Google loses information needed to determine if disclosing confidential information violates EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 1 introduces EAR under Title 15 Commerce and Foreign Trade, Chapter VII Bureau of Industry and Security, Subchapter C Export Administration Regulations. By removing the citizenship certification, Google increases the risk of violating export controls, which presents a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Participant will use the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Participant uses with respect to its own similar information to protect the Confidential Information and to prevent (i) any use of Confidential Information not authorized in this Agreement, (ii) dissemination of Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant will use a reasonable degree of care to protect the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Participant uses with respect to its own similar information' weakens the protection afforded to Google's Confidential Information. This removes a contractual obligation for the participant to exercise the same level of diligence they use for their own sensitive information, and exposes Google to legal risk if the participant is negligent.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714 establishes the general duty of care. While a 'reasonable degree of care' aligns with this, the original clause contractually reinforces the duty of care, aligning it with the Participant's own practices for similar information. Removing this creates a weaker standard of care, making it potentially more difficult for Google to prove negligence if the Confidential Information is mishandled. By weakening the standard below what the Participant uses to protect their *own* similar information, the agreement could be seen as enabling a lower standard than would be considered reasonable, thus potentially contradicting the implied duty of care.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract's standard of care, potentially undermining the level of protection afforded to confidential information. The original text explicitly requires the participant to use 'the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Participant uses with respect to its own similar information.' The changed text only requires 'a reasonable degree of care.' California Civil Code § 1714(a) states, 'Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill.' While the changed text stipulates 'reasonable care,' removing the comparison to the Participant's own practices weakens the protection and could be interpreted as a lower standard than what is reasonable, especially if the Participant safeguards its own information with a higher degree of diligence.
